tutorial_ruins_invitation.name: Adventurer
lines: Hey! I haven't seen you here before.
Are you a new adventurer? If you are, you should totally explore the ruins to my right.
The rest of my party is already inside. I think they would be willing to help you if you need it.

tutorial_ruins_about_elevator.name: Adventurer
lines: What am I doing? I am trying to analyze this ancient lamp.
These old contraptions truly are interesting...
That elevator for example; how does it still work after all this time?

tutorial_ruins_deploy_puzzle_hint.name: Adventurer
lines: You want to know my thoughts on this puzzle? It certainly is interesting.
Based on what I can tell, you need to place a robot onto one of the buttons to open the first door.
To achieve this, you can open your inventory (Tab), select your robot and deploy it.
The door after it then only opens when there are no robots on any of the buttons.
If you configure the robot (E while near it), you can destroy it and pick it back up.

tutorial_ruins_first_program_puzzle_hint.name: Adventurer
lines: It looks like here we only need to move a robot onto the button.
However, we are only allowed to deploy robots in the area shown by the hammers.
I guess that means we have to program the robot to move onto the button.
Once you have a robot deployed, you configure it (E while near it) and use left click to add a processor to the modules.

You can then program the robot (C while near it) and write:

move(right)
Then you just need to start the robot, possibly several times.
Don't forget to take the robot with you when you're done!

tutorial_ruins_loop_puzzle_hint.name: Adventurer
lines: This puzzle looks similar to the previous one, doesn't it?
Looks can be deceiving though. See those red antennas? Your robot cannot stop inside of that area.

tutorial_ruins_follow_puzzle_hint.name: Adventurer
lines: For this puzzle the robot needs to move for a very long time without stopping.
If only there was a way for the robot to follow the player?

I think the robot would move towards the player if one wrote something like this:

move(playerDir())
Maybe this could be combined with a loop?

merchant_greeting_1.name: Merchant
lines: Anything that catches your eye?
@OPEN_SHOP:spawn_town

merchant_greeting_2.name: Merchant
lines: Do you have something specific in mind, or are you just browsing?
@OPEN_SHOP:spawn_town

combat_ruins_warning.name: Adventurer
lines: Hey, are you really sure you wanna go in there?
Be prepared to fight a lot of uncontrolled robots...

bigton_hint_laser.name: Adventurer - About Lasers
lines: What's up? You want to know how to use the laser?
Well, it's actually not that complicated! You can shoot the laser in any given direction by using the "laserShoot"-function.

For example, to shoot the laser towards the left, you can write:

laserShoot(left)
However, the laser has a cooldown, during which "laserShoot" does nothing.

bigton_hint_radar.name: Adventurer - About Radars
lines: You need help with the radar module? Yeah that one took me a while to figure out too.

To start using it, you first need to tell the radar module to search for robots nearby. You can do this by writing:

radarSearch()

The "radarSearch"-function results in the number of robots that were found, so you can check how many robots there are in the radar area:

print(radarSearch())
Once you have used the "radarSearch"-function, you can use "radarNext" to select each of the found robots. It returns 1 if the next robot was selected, or 0 if no more robots have been found.

bigton_hint_sonar.name: Adventurer - About Sonars
lines: The sonar module? I don't use it much, but if I remember correctly it's not actually that complicated.
I am pretty sure there is a "sonarDist" function that takes a direction and returns the number of tiles that the robot could move in that direction before hitting an obstacle.

For example, to check how much space there is towards the right, I would write something like:

print(sonarDist(right))

bigton_hint_async_movement.name: Adventurer - About "moveAsync"
lines: Are you also confused about the "move"-function blocking your program until the robot has stopped moving?
I recently learned that there exists another function called "moveAsync", which doesn't do this!
"moveAsync" does the same thing as "move", but keeps the program runnning. However, "moveAsync" and "move" do nothing while the robot is already moving.
By the way, you can use the "canMove"-function to check if the robot can be moved. It returns 1 if the robot is standing still, and 0 if it is already moving.

bigton_hint_tick.name: Adventurer - About "tick"-loops
lines: Are you worried about your programs using up 100% CPU time?
Well, you are probably writing your robot code in a "loop", which repeats the code inside as fast as possible.
However, you can use a "tick"-loop, which is very similar to a "loop", but simply waits a short time before repeating the code.
This makes it so your processor isn't as busy all the time.
The exact frequency? If I had to guess, I would probably say a "tick" loop repeats somewhere around 20 times per second.

bigton_hint_cardinals.name: Adventurer - About Cardinal Directions
lines: You are confused about directions when writing code? Understandable, they are not that intuitive.
Basically, when you write "left" in your code, that gets replaced with a pair of numbers, in this case (-1, 0).
This pair of numbers represents the direction; the first number the number of horizontal steps, the second number the number of vertical steps.
Negative numbers point left and up, positive numbers point right and down.
These numbers don't always need to be -1, 0 or 1 though. If they are however, we call that direction a cardinal direction, as it must either be "left", "right", "up", "down" or "here".
For example, "playerDir" or "radarRobotDir" don't always return cardinal directions, but simply the number of horizontal and vertical steps.
"toCardinal" takes a direction and returns the cardinal direction that most closely matches the input.
"toLesserCardinal" takes a direction and returns another cardinal direction that most closely matches the input, not including the one returned by "toCardinal".
